questions.question: Explain the concept of a write-back cache policy and contrast it with a write-through policy. In your answer, describe one advantage and one disadvantage of the write-back approach in a parallel computing environment.
keyPoints: Definition: Data is written only to the cache initially; the main memory is updated later when the cache block is replaced.
Contrast: Unlike write-through, which updates main memory on every write, write-back delays the memory write.
Advantage: Reduces traffic to main memory, decreasing contention and improving performance.
Disadvantage: Creates complexity for cache coherency protocols, as other processors may have stale data.

questions.question: Define False Sharing and explain why it is detrimental to performance in a shared-memory multiprocessor system.
keyPoints: Definition: Occurs when two or more processors frequently access different variables that reside on the same cache line.
Performance Impact: The cache coherency protocol invalidates the entire cache line for all processors on a write, even if the written data is unrelated.
Result: Causes unnecessary cache misses and forces other processors to re-load the cache line from memory, creating a bottleneck.

questions.question: Describe the MESI (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) cache coherency protocol. For each state, explain the meaning and the permissions (read/write) a processor has for a cache line in that state.
keyPoints: Modified (M): The cache line is dirty (modified) and is the only valid copy; the processor has both read and write permissions.
Exclusive (E): The cache line is clean (matches main memory) and is the only copy; the processor has read and write permissions.
Shared (S): The cache line is clean and may exist in other caches; the processor has read permission only (must invalidate others to write).
Invalid (I): The cache line contains no valid data; the processor has no permissions and must fetch a new copy.
